19.text: Quantization changes representation, not the model's architecture, so the ordinary transformer math remains the same around the compression step.

20.text: Same shared corpus again. That keeps the lesson focused on compression and inference cost rather than on task differences.

21.text: We still control the run setup because we want to compare FP32 and INT8 behavior on equal footing.

22.text: Timing is part of the point here: quantization is valuable because smaller weights are cheaper to move through memory, not just because they save disk space.

24.text: PyTorch handles training in normal precision first; only after that do we compress the learned weights into a cheaper format.

25.text: The same familiar layers will later be wrapped or replaced with quantized versions so you can see exactly where the memory savings come from.

26.text: The model's behavior should stay approximately the same after quantization, so the surrounding functional operations are intentionally unchanged.

28.text: Lock the RNGs so any quality drop after quantization reflects the compressed weights, not a different training run.

29.text: Set the PyTorch random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

34.text: Build a portable file path to the dataset, relative to this script's location.

35.text: Download the dataset from the internet if it is not already on disk.

41.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace.

42.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

45.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters in the dataset to form the token vocabulary.

46.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID.

47.text: The full vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS marker.

53.text: Larger model than lab 03 (64 vs 16 embedding dim) to make quantization effects visible. At 16 dimensions the savings are tiny; at 64 the 4x compression is meaningful.

56.text: Names rarely exceed 16 characters, so this caps the positional embedding table and keeps sequences compact.

57.text: Each attention head operates on a 16-dimensional slice of the 64-dimensional embedding.

60.text: Root-mean-square normalization that stabilizes activations without learned parameters.

66.text: Scale the input by its inverse root-mean-square, ensuring activations have roughly unit magnitude without learned parameters.

69.text: Standard causal attention, same as lab 03.

72.text: Q, K, V, output projections. These are the weights that will be quantized to INT8.

73.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into key vectors for attention.

74.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into value vectors for attention.

75.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension.

77.text: Run the attention computation: project to Q, K, V, apply the causal mask, and combine.

78.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input tensor shape.

79.text: Project and reshape the input into query vectors, one per attention head.

80.text: Project and reshape the input into key vectors, one per attention head.

81.text: Project and reshape the input into value vectors, one per attention head.

83.text: Compute Q dot K transposed, scaled by sqrt(head_dim). The scaling prevents softmax from saturating as dimensionality grows.

84.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask so each token can only attend to itself and earlier positions.

85.text: Set all future positions to negative infinity so softmax assigns them zero weight, enforcing the causal left-to-right constraint.

86.text: Convert attention scores into a probability distribution that sums to one over the key dimension.

88.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors and reshape. Each position now holds a context-aware blend of information from earlier tokens.

89.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension through a linear layer.

92.text: A standard two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation in between.

96.text: Compress the expanded 256-dimensional representation back to the 64-dimensional embedding.

98.text: Run the input through the two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation.

99.text: The complete feed-forward computation: expand to 4x width, apply ReLU nonlinearity, and compress back in a single expression.

102.text: One transformer block containing self-attention and a feed-forward network with residual connections.

105.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

106.text: The causal self-attention sublayer that lets each token attend to previous positions.

107.text: Normalize activations before the MLP sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

108.text: The feed-forward network that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information across positions.

110.text: Apply attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections that let gradients flow easily.

111.text: Add the attention output to the residual stream, preserving the original information.

112.text: Add the MLP output to the residual stream so the model can refine representations incrementally.

116.text: Full model, same architecture as lab 03 but larger (64-dim instead of 16-dim).

119.text: Look up learned token embeddings in a 27 by 64 table, one vector per token.

120.text: Look up learned position embeddings in a 16 by 64 table, one vector per position.

121.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer.

122.text: A stack of transformer blocks, each containing attention and MLP sublayers.

124.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

128.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian so activations stay reasonably scaled at the start.

131.text: Run the full model on the input sequence to produce next-token logits for every position.

132.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

133.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

134.text: Create a position embedding for each slot in the sequence so the model knows token ordering.

135.text: Add token and position embeddings together, then normalize before entering the transformer.

136.text: Pass the normalized representation through each transformer block in sequence.

138.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

144.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

145.text: Instantiate the model and move it to the target device for training.

148.text: Set up the Adam optimizer with momentum and epsilon tuned for this small model.

149.text: Train for 1000 steps, enough for this model to learn basic character-level name patterns.

151.text: Standard training loop, identical to other labs.

152.text: Pick the next training document by cycling through the shuffled name list.

153.text: Encode each character in the name as a token ID, bookended by BOS markers.

154.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits the model's positional embedding table.

156.text: Convert the first n token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as input.

157.text: Build the target sequence by shifting the input one position to the right for next-token prediction.

159.text: Run the full model on the current input batch to get next-token predictions.

160.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy loss.

162.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

163.text: Compute gradients for every parameter by propagating the loss backward through the graph.

164.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

165.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it on the next step.

167.text: Apply the optimizer step, adjusting every parameter in the direction that reduces the loss.

178.text: The quantized linear layer: stores weights as INT8 integers (1 byte each) instead of FP32 floats (4 bytes each). A single scale factor maps between the two representations.

179.text: Takes a trained FP32 linear layer and quantizes its weights.

181.text: Detach the FP32 weight tensor from the computation graph since we only need the raw numbers for the quantization math.

182.text: Compute the scale factor: map the weight range [-max, +max] to [-127, +127]. This single scalar is the key, it determines the precision of the quantization.

183.text: Round to nearest integer and cast to INT8. Each weight now fits in 1 byte instead of 4. The register_buffer saves it as a non-parameter tensor (no gradients).

184.text: Keep the scale factor as a buffer so we can undo the quantization during inference by multiplying INT8 values back to float.

185.text: Carry over bias if the original layer had one (ours don't).

187.text: Forward: dequantize on-the-fly by casting back to float and multiplying by scale. In production, INT8 matmul kernels avoid this cast entirely, they compute directly in integer arithmetic.

188.text: Cast INT8 to float, multiply by scale, then do the matrix multiply. Mathematically: x @ (W_int8 * scale)^T. The approximation error is small because neural network weights are typically well-distributed.

191.text: Quantize the entire model: create a fresh copy and replace all Linear layers with QuantizedLinear.

192.text: Instantiate a fresh model with the same architecture. We'll load trained weights into it and then swap its Linear layers for quantized ones.

195.text: Look for Linear layers to quantize.

196.text: Found one, replace with quantized version.

198.text: Copy the trained FP32 weights into the new model so we preserve learned behavior before replacing layers with quantized versions.

199.text: Walk every module in the model.

205.text: Controls how peaked the sampling distribution is. Lower temperature means more deterministic output; higher means more creative variety.

207.text: Decorator: no gradient tracking needed for inference.

208.text: Generate names, same function for both FP32 and INT8 models.

211.text: Loop through each sample, generating one name at a time from the model.

212.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

214.text: Feed the current token sequence through the model to get next-token logits, keeping only the last block_size tokens.

215.text: Temperature-scaled softmax and sample in one line.

216.text: Randomly draw one token from the probability distribution for stochastic generation.

217.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

218.text: Switch to evaluation mode so the model produces consistent outputs during generation.

219.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and continue generating the next one.

224.text: Benchmark: measure model size on disk and generation speed.

225.text: Temp file import for saving the model.

228.text: Write the model's state dict to a temporary file on disk so we can measure its serialized size in bytes.

229.text: Serialize the state dict, this is what you'd ship to production.

230.text: File size in MB, INT8 should be roughly 4x smaller.

231.text: Time 100 generations for a stable measurement.

232.text: Generate 100 names in a batch to get a stable timing measurement for the benchmark.

234.text: Package the file size in MB and average time per generation into a tuple for the benchmark comparison table.

239.text: Quantize the trained model to INT8.

240.text: Run the same benchmark suite on the INT8 model to compare size and speed against the original FP32 baseline.

254.text: Side-by-side comparison loop for FP32 and INT8 samples. Each model will be sampled under the same random seed so differences come from the weights, not different random draws.

255.text: Reset the random seed before sampling each model, then print the label. This makes the sample comparison reproducible and directly comparable.

256.text: Generate 10 samples from the current model under that fixed seed.

257.text: Decode and print each generated name.
